core_claim: Kimi Code CLI is a terminal AI coding agent with shell, file, and web access, so the real security risk is not the model but the permissions you grant and the untrusted content it acts on.

slides.on_slide_copy: YOUR CODING AGENT CAN RUN COMMANDS A WEB PAGE TOLD IT TO
slides.subline: Kimi Code CLI is a terminal agent with shell access. The model is fine. The permissions are the risk.

slides.on_slide_copy: The agent treats the text it fetches as instructions, not just data, so a poisoned page can tell it to act.

slides.on_slide_copy: Teams run it on their main machine with real keys, then flip on the no-prompt mode for speed.
slides.subline: MCP servers and plugins from any repo run in your trust context too.

slides.on_slide_copy: Run it in a throwaway VM with restricted network egress and no real credentials, and keep the approve-before-run gate on.
slides.subline: Only add MCP servers and plugins you have pinned and actually read.

caption:
Moonshot shipped Kimi Code CLI, a terminal AI coding agent written in TypeScript. It reads your code, runs shell commands, and fetches web pages, then it picks the next step on its own. It is MIT licensed and genuinely useful.

Here is the part the launch posts skip. An agent that reads the open web and then runs your shell can be steered by the content it reads. A poisoned page or README turns into instructions, and the payoff is your files, your keys, and your machine. The default build asks before it edits or runs. The skip-all-approvals mode throws that gate away.

Treat it like untrusted code, because that is what it executes. Run it in a throwaway VM with restricted network egress and no real credentials. Keep the approve-before-run gate on outside of disposable boxes. Only load MCP servers and plugins you have pinned and actually read.

The risk was never the model. It is every command and secret you let the agent reach.

Would you give a terminal agent your shell without a sandbox?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Would you run a terminal coding agent on your main machine, or only in a sandbox?
alt_text: Slide 1 (cover): a sleek robotic hand resting on a heavy metal lever in the dark, poised to pull it, with the headline in the lower third.
Slide 2 (context): a robotic arm lifting a single glowing key out of an open box in a dark scene.
Slide 3 (risk): a puppet control bar with taut strings descending into darkness toward an unseen puppet, one string glowing.
Slide 4 (mechanism): a folded paper note slipped under a heavy closed door, light leaking through the gap.
Slide 5 (failure_point): a ring of keys left in an unlocked door while a hand reaches past a small ignored warning tag.
Slide 6 (defense): a robotic arm working inside a sealed glass box with a padlock on the lid.
Slide 7 (takeaway): a single iron key held in deep shadow with a thin rim of light along its edge.
Slide 8 (cta): a closed padlock resting in empty dark space, with the comment question and handle.

sources.source: MarkTechPost: Moonshot AI Releases Kimi Code CLI
sources.supports: Kimi Code CLI is a terminal AI coding agent built in TypeScript by Moonshot AI for next-gen agents.
Capabilities (reads and edits code, runs shell commands, searches files, fetches web pages, chooses next step), MIT license, MCP and plugin support.
Read-only actions run automatically; file edits and shell commands require confirmation by default; a skip-all-approvals mode exists.

narration.text: Moonshot just put an AI coding agent in your terminal, and it can run your shell.
Kimi Code CLI reads files, runs commands, and fetches web pages, then it decides the next step on its own.
So treat it like untrusted code. Run it in a throwaway VM with no real keys, and keep the approve-before-run gate on.
The risk was never the model. It is every command and secret you let the agent reach.
Would you hand a terminal agent your shell without a sandbox? Tell me below.

The risk was never the model. It is every command and secret you let the agent reach.
